title: Equivariant Kuranishi structure and its (potential) applications
speaker: Kenji Fukaya
tags: Homological Mirror Symmetry: Methods and Structures

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian reduction and equivariant Fukaya input
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a Hamiltonian action of a compact Lie group on a symplectic manifold, a free regular symplectic quotient, G-equivariant Lagrangians in the zero level, and the simplified equivariant Fukaya/Kuranishi operations obtained by quotienting the relevant disc moduli spaces.

proof_stages.label: Bulk-deformed comparison theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main claimed theorem is that the simplified G-equivariant Fukaya category upstairs is equivalent to the Fukaya category of the quotient after a canonically chosen positive-energy bulk deformation, with the relation to gauged sigma-model approaches discussed for context.

proof_stages.label: Applications to potentials and unobstructedness
proof_stages.summary: The comparison is applied to toric quotients and torus fibers, where the upstairs leading-order potential is easy to compute and the theorem predicts a downstairs bulk deformation cancelling extra terms and transferring weak unobstructedness.

proof_stages.label: Lagrangian-correspondence bimodule moduli spaces
proof_stages.summary: To prove the theorem, the speaker introduces strip-like moduli spaces of pairs of maps to X and to the quotient Y with seam matching along mu^{-1}(0)/G, marked points on both boundary components, a G-action, quotient evaluation maps, and resulting operations n_{k1,k2}.

proof_stages.label: Cyclic A-infinity bimodule criterion
proof_stages.summary: An algebraic lemma is recalled: an A-infinity bimodule between two A-infinity algebras with a cyclic element identifies their Maurer-Cartan moduli, hence transfers unobstructedness, once the necessary bulk-corrected bimodule relations hold.

proof_stages.label: Boundary analysis and seam-bubble obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The codimension-one compactification boundary is analyzed: ordinary disc bubbling and splitting give exactly the A-infinity bimodule relations, but additional bubbles along the middle seam break those relations and express the usual obstruction to functoriality for a Lagrangian correspondence.

proof_stages.label: Bulk unobstruction of the correspondence
proof_stages.summary: For the specific correspondence L_{XY}={(x,y): mu(x)=0 and y=x/G}, the speaker invokes the general obstruction theory for Lagrangians modulo ambient cohomology and reviews bulk-deformed operations via interior marked points constrained to a bulk cycle.

proof_stages.label: Energy-inductive cancellation of seam bubbles
proof_stages.summary: The remaining seam-bubble error is killed inductively in energy: the smallest-energy bubble produces an obstruction cycle in Y, the next term of the bulk class is chosen as its negative, constant bulk-insertion configurations cancel it, and Gromov compactness permits repeating this to obtain the required bimodule and comparison.

examples.name: Gauged sigma-model comparison
examples.note: Gauged holomorphic maps with principal G-bundles and connections are cited as a related framework, with the trivial-bundle part resembling the upstairs theory and twisted bundles heuristically corresponding to the bulk correction.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only contrasts his approach with gauged sigma models by describing their general bundle/connection setup and suggesting a relation to bulk classes, without carrying out the comparison concretely

examples.name: Toric symplectic quotient and Landau-Ginzburg potential
examples.note: The quotient of a complex vector space by a torus, with Lagrangian torus fibers, illustrates how the upstairs leading-order potential is finite and how an appropriate bulk class downstairs cancels extra non-Fano corrections.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the main theorem to the toric quotient case, writes the superpotential as a sum of A-infinity operations, compares it with the upstairs leading-order potential, and explains how a bulk class cancels extra terms

passes.reason: a toric symplectic quotient is a systematic maximally torus-symmetric family used as the main illustrative application, not as a refutation
A toric quotient/Landau-Ginzburg potential is a highly torus-symmetric illustrative application, not a refutation of a claim.
Toric symplectic quotients/Landau–Ginzburg potentials are used as a standard symmetric test/application case, not to refute a claim.
A toric symplectic quotient is used as a standard illustrative application and has maximal torus symmetry.
toric symplectic quotients are maximally torus-symmetric standard examples used to illustrate the bulk-deformed Landau-Ginzburg potential comparison.

Used as an illustrative application where a toric quotient/Landau-Ginzburg potential is compared under bulk deformation, and toric quotients have maximal torus symmetry.
Used as a basic application/test case; toric quotients form a systematic maximally torus-symmetric family.

examples.name: Lipyanskiy's ASD/homorphic-curve matching condition
examples.note: The speaker mentions Lipyanskiy's use of analogous seam-matching conditions between anti-self-dual connections and holomorphic curves to make the Lagrangian-correspondence moduli space more familiar.
examples.engagement_reason: Lipyanskiy’s ASD/holomorphic-curve matching condition is cited as a similar construction, but the speaker does not work through that example itself
